oak: Noakhailla is an eastern Indo-Aryan language spoken by about 14 million people mainly in the Greater Noakhali District in the Chittagong Division in the southeast of Bangladesh, and in the south of Tripura state in the northeast of India.
cmn:
Mandarin Chinese is the world's most widely spoken native language, serving as the official and national language of the People's Republic of China and Taiwan, and as one of the four official languages of Singapore. As a principal member of the Sinitic language family, it is characterized by its complex and highly nuanced tonal system where the meaning of a word can change entirely based on its specific pitch and inflection. This unique linguistic feature, combined with a profound historical lineage that spans thousands of years, makes Mandarin one of the most intellectually fascinating and culturally influential languages in human history, serving as the primary vehicle for the philosophy, poetry, and administration of one of the world's oldest continuous civilizations.

Historically, Mandarin evolved from the local dialects of North China and became established as the language of the imperial court as early as the Ming Dynasty, eventually being codified as the standard national language in the early 20th century. The term 'Mandarin' itself has its origins in the Portuguese word 'mandarim,' which referred to the high-ranking Chinese officials with whom early European traders and explorers interacted during their travels. Over many centuries, it has served as the administrative and literary standard for the vast Chinese empire, unifying an incredibly diverse geographical area and hundreds of millions of people under a single, sophisticated linguistic umbrella that continues to define East Asian geopolitics to this day.

Today, Mandarin is the primary medium of education, mass media, and government across mainland China and Taiwan, and it is written using a system of logographic characters known as Hanzi, which boast a history spanning over 3,000 years. These characters serve as a vital cultural bridge, allowing speakers of mutually unintelligible Chinese varieties--such as Cantonese or Shanghainese-to communicate effectively through writing. The monumental transition from Traditional to Simplified characters in mainland China during the 1950s was a critical historical event intended to rapidly increase literacy among the general population, although Traditional characters remain the standard in Taiwan, Hong Kong, and many overseas Chinese communities globally.

With the rapid rise of China as a leading global economic and technological superpower in the 21st century, the importance of Mandarin has significantly transcended its national borders, becoming an essential language for international business, scientific research, and global diplomacy. Educational institutions on every continent have seen a massive surge in Mandarin learners, reflecting its status as an indispensable tool for engaging with the future of global politics, economics, and cultural exchange. As we move deeper into an interconnected age, Mandarin remains at the forefront of global communication, carrying with it the weight of an ancient heritage into a high-tech, globalized future that it is increasingly helping to shape.
spa:
Spanish, also frequently referred to as Castilian, is a global Romance language that originated in the Castile region of Spain and has since expanded through centuries of history to become the second most spoken native language in the entire world. With over 490 million people identifying it as their primary mother tongue as of 2026, Spanish serves as the official language of 20 sovereign states and is widely spoken across Europe, the Americas, and parts of both Africa and Asia. Its incredible geographical reach and the sheer number of its speakers make it one of the most important languages for global interaction, commerce, and the preservation of a diverse and vibrant cultural heritage that spans the Atlantic.

The historical roots of Spanish lie deep in the Vulgar Latin brought to the Iberian Peninsula by the Roman Empire more than two millennia ago. Following the eventual fall of the Western Roman Empire, the language evolved distinctively in the northern regions of Spain, heavily influenced by diversos local languages and significantly by Arabic during the many centuries of Moorish rule in the peninsula. This complex and fascinating historical blending created a language that is remarkably phonetic, melodic, and expressive, possessing a rich and diverse vocabulary that reflects the intricate maritime and land-based history of both the Mediterranean and the Atlantic worlds.

Spanish literature is among the most celebrated and studied in the world, boasting a prestigious tradition that ranges from the foundational 17th-century masterpiece 'Don Quixote' by Miguel de Cervantes to the groundbreaking Magical Realism of 20th-century Nobel laureate Gabriel Garcia Marquez The language serves as a powerful vehicle for a vibrant, global culture that is expressed through world-class music, award-winning cinema, and deeply rooted traditional arts. In the modern era, Spanish continues to grow in influence and population, particularly in the United States, where it is now the most widely spoken language after English and plays a crucial role in the country's social, political, and economic life.

The global reach and standardization of Spanish are meticulously managed by the Real Academia Espanola (RAE) in Madrid and its many sister academies in other Spanish-speaking nations, ensuring a remarkable degree of mutual intelligibility despite the vast distances and cultural differences between its speakers. Whether it is encountered in its European, Latin American, Caribbean, or Philippine forms, Spanish remains a dynamic, unified, and deeply expressive force for hundreds of millions of people. As the world becomes more integrated, the role of Spanish as a bridge between the Old World and the New World, and between diverse cultures across the globe, only continues to strengthen, marking it as a truly indispensable global tongue.
eng:
English is a West Germanic language that first emerged in early medieval England and has since evolved through a series of dramatic historical shifts into the world's most significant and dominant global lingua franca. Driven by the massive historical expansion of the British Empire during the 18th and 19th centuries, and subsequently by the overwhelming economic, scientific, and cultural reach of the United States, English is now spoken by more than 1.5 billion people worldwide. This vast community includes native speakers, those who use it as a primary second language, and millions of others who learn it as a vital tool for international communication and professional advancement.

The history of the English language is a fascinating and often turbulent journey of linguistic transformation and adaptation. It began as a collection of closely related dialects brought by Germanic tribes--specifically the Angles, Saxons, and Jutes-to the British Isles from northern Europe. The language was later profoundly reshaped by the Viking invasions, which introduced Old Norse influences, and most dramatically by the Norman Conquest of 1066. The latter event introduced thousands of French and Latin words into the vocabulary, creating the unique 'Middle English' that eventually transitioned into the modern form we recognize today, characterized by a vast and uniquely flexible vocabulary.

In the 21st century, English has solidified its position as the undisputed primary language of international business, academic research, commercial aviation, and advanced digital technology. It is the overwhelming dominant language of the internet, with a vast majority of global data and content produced in English. This status as an international standard enables people from incredibly diverse linguistic and cultural backgrounds to communicate effectively in a globalized world, though this success has also led to the fascinating rise of numerous distinct regional varieties, such as Indian English, Nigerian English, and the creolized 'Singlish' of Singapore.

Despite its global dominance, the English language is frequently noted by learners for its highly irregular spelling and often bewilderingly complex phonology, which are the direct results of its multi-layered history. However, its remarkable flexibility and its constant, open-ended assimilation of new words from nearly every other language on Earth continue to make it a living, breathing entity that rapidly adapts to the changing needs of its speakers. As we move further into the interconnected age of the 2026 Ethnologue 29th Edition, English remains the indispensable and most powerful tool for connecting the world's diverse cultures, innovative ideas, and disparate peoples across every continent.
hin:
Hindi is an Indo-Aryan language spoken primarily in the vast 'Hindi Belt' of northern and central India, where it serves as one of the two official languages of the Union. According to the 2026 Ethnologue data, it is the fourth most spoken native language in the world and acts as the primary lingua franca for hundreds of millions of people across the sprawling Indian subcontinent. Written in the elegant Devanagari script, Hindi is not just a method of communication but a language of immense historical, religious, and cultural depth, carrying the weight of ancient traditions into a rapidly modernizing South Asia.

Modern Standard Hindi is primarily based on the Khariboli dialect of the Delhi region and has been significantly enriched through centuries of influence from Sanskrit, Persian, and Arabic. Its classical and modern literary tradition dates back many centuries, featuring profound poetry and philosophy that have shaped the Indian mind. After India achieved independence in 1947, Hindi was prominently promoted as a national symbol of unity and cultural self-reliance. Since that time, it has become the dominant language of administration, law, education, and mass media in several of India's largest and most influential states, including Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, and Rajasthan.

One of the most powerful and visible drivers of Hindi's global influence in the modern era is the massive Indian film industry, popularly known worldwide as Bollywood. Hindi cinema, with its distinctive storytelling and iconic musical soundtracks, is celebrated and consumed by audiences across the globe, from the Middle East and Russia to North Africa and the West. This cultural export has played a vital role in making Hindi widely understood and appreciated even in regions where it is not the primary native language, such as in the southern states of India and in neighboring countries like Pakistan, Nepal, and Mauritius.

As India continues its path of rapid economic and technological development in the 2020s, Hindi is constantly evolving to incorporate new terminology from the worlds of technology, social media, and modern lifestyles, often resulting in the popular and highly expressive hybrid known as 'Hinglish.' While maintaining its deep ancient roots in the Sanskrit tradition, the language stands as a powerful testament to the dynamic and incredibly diverse nature of Indian society. It serves as an essential bridge between India's traditional intellectual heritage and its vibrant, high-tech, and globalized future, ensuring that the voice of the world's most populous nation continues to resonate clearly on the global stage.
por:
Portuguese is a major global Romance language that originated in the western part of the Iberian Peninsula and has since grown into a massive international communication tool with over 240 million native speakers. It is currently the official language of sovereign nations spanning four different continents, including Portugal in Europe, Brazil in South America, and several countries in Africa such as Angola, Mozambique, and Cape Verde. This wide distribution makes Portuguese the most spoken language across the entire Southern Hemisphere and the undisputed most spoken language in South America, reflecting its status as a core component of the global linguistic map.

The history of Portuguese is inextricably tied to the 'Age of Discovery' in the 15th and 16th centuries, a period when Portuguese explorers first mapped much of the known world. This era not only spread the language to far-flung corners of the Earth but also introduced a wealth of words from Asian, African, and Indigenous American languages into the Portuguese lexicon, creating a truly globalized vocabulary. The language originally evolved from the Galician-Portuguese spoken during the Middle Ages, gradually attaining its own distinct phonetic and grammatical identity as Portugal established itself as a pioneering and independent maritime kingdom.

Brazil, as the world's largest Portuguese-speaking nation by a significant margin, has developed its own highly distinctive and internationally recognized variety of the language. Brazilian Portuguese is famous for its melodic cadence, open vowels, and highly expressive vocabulary, which are felt globally through the influence of Bossa Nova music, Samba, and the widespread popularity of Brazilian cinema and literature. Meanwhile, European Portuguese maintains a more conservative phonology and serves as the standard for the language's original roots in the Old World, with both varieties continuing to influence each other through modern digital media and shared cultural initiatives.

In the year 2026, Portuguese is a highly strategic language for international trade, energy, and global diplomacy, particularly within the framework of the Community of Portuguese Language Countries (CPLP). Its widespread and strategic geographical distribution ensures that it remains a vital link between the economies of Europe, Africa, and the Americas. For its hundreds of millions of speakers, Portuguese represents not just a rich shared history of exploration and resilience, but a vibrant and increasingly influential future in a world where the voice of the Lusophone community continues to grow in both cultural and economic importance.
ben:
Bengali, also extensively known by its endonym Bangla, is a major Indo-Aryan language native to the historic Bengal region of South Asia, which today comprises the independent nation of Bangladesh and the Indian state of West Bengal. It is the national and official language of Bangladesh and one of the most prominent of the 22 scheduled languages of India. With over 236 million native speakers as recorded in the 2026 Ethnologue 29th Edition, Bengali comfortably sits as one of the six or seven most spoken languages in the entire world, representing a massive and culturally cohesive population.

Bengali is written in the unique and visually striking Bengali-Assamese script, a beautiful system of writing that has supported a sophisticated and diverse literary tradition for well over a thousand years. The language is an immense source of national and cultural pride for its speakers, particularly following the historic Bengali Language Movement of 1952. During this period, students and activists in what was then East Pakistan sacrificed their lives to protect the status of their mother tongue against linguistic imposition. This epoch-defining event eventually led to the independence of Bangladesh and is now commemorated annually across the globe as International Mother Language Day.

The vast literary heritage of Bengali is dominated by the monumental figure of Rabindranath Tagore, who in 1913 became the first non-European to be awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature. His poetry, music (known as Rabindra Sangeet), and prose continue to define the cultural and intellectual landscape of the entire Bengal region. Other literary giants, such as Kazi Nazrul Islam, the 'Rebel Poet,' have used the language to express powerful themes of social justice, anti-colonialism, and revolution, making Bengali a language that is both profoundly beautiful in its aesthetics and fierce in its political and social power.

In the current digital age, Bengali is a dynamic and rapidly growing language, supported by both a massive home population and a large, influential global diaspora. It is the primary medium for a thriving film industry centered in Kolkata (Tollywood), a vibrant and ancient folk music tradition, and a highly sophisticated academic and scientific environment in Bangladesh and India. As the economy of the Bengal region continues to expand, the language adapts seamlessly to the requirements of the 21st century, remaining a vital, resilient, and vibrant expression of one of the world's most populous and intellectually rich cultural spheres.
rus:
Russian is a major East Slavic language that serves as the official and national language of Russia, Belarus, Kazakhstan, and Kyrgyzstan, and remains widely spoken and understood throughout Eastern Europe, the Baltic states, and much of Central Asia. It is the most spoken native language in all of Europe and the most geographically widespread language across the vast territory of Eurasia. As the core language of the former Soviet Union and the Russian Empire before it, it continues to be a vital tool for cross-border communication, trade, and diplomacy across a massive and diverse portion of the globe.

Written in the distinctive Cyrillic alphabet, the development of the Russian language is deeply intertwined with the history of the Russian Orthodox Church and the centuries-long expansion of the Russian state from a collection of principalities into a global power. The language reached a world-class literary and classical peak in the 19th century, a period famously known as the 'Golden Age of Russian Literature.' Masterpieces produced during this time by legendary authors such as Alexander Pushkin, Leo Tolstoy, Fyodor Dostoevsky, and Anton Chekhov have become essential cornerstones of world literature, celebrated for their immense philosophical depth and their penetrating exploration of the human soul.

Russian also holds a prestigious position as a primary language of international science, mathematics, and high-tech engineering. Most notably, it was the first language ever spoken in space, uttered by the pioneer Yuri Gagarin in 1961, and it remains one of the two essential working languages for the International Space Station to this day. Its status as one of the six official languages of the United Nations further underscores its enduring importance in global diplomacy, international security, and the complex landscape of 21st-century international relations.

Despite the significant political and social changes of recent decades, Russian remains a formidable and unifying cultural force for millions of people living both within and outside of Russia. Its complex grammar, featuring a rich system of cases and suffixes, allows for an incredible degree of semantic nuance and expressive power, making it a favorite medium for poets, philosophers, and scientists alike. As a living language, it continues to evolve rapidly, absorbing modern global influences while stubbornly maintaining the historical strength and poetic beauty for which it has been recognized worldwide for centuries.
jpn:
Japanese is a Japonic language spoken by over 124 million people, primarily within the island nation of Japan, where it serves as the sole national and official language. It is famous among linguists for its unique structure, particularly its highly complex system of social honorifics known as 'keigo,' which reflects the deep-seated cultural importance of hierarchy, social status, and relative politeness in Japanese society. This intricate and often subtle relationship between the language and the cultural values of its speakers makes Japanese a subject of intense fascination and study for people all over the world.

The Japanese writing system is widely considered one of the most complex and visually beautiful in modern use, remarkably employing three distinct scripts in tandem: Kanji (Chinese-derived logographic characters), Hiragana, and Katakana. Kanji, originally brought to Japan from the Asian mainland in the 5th century, are used to represent core concepts and roots, while the phonetic Hiragana and Katakana scripts are used for grammatical elements and foreign loanwords, respectively. This sophisticated combination allows for an incredible degree of precision and aesthetics in written communication, which is celebrated in traditional arts such as Shodo (calligraphy).

Japanese culture possesses a massive and growing global reach in the 2020s, primarily through the overwhelming international success of anime, manga, and video games. This pop-culture phenomenon has introduced the Japanese language to hundreds of millions of people across the globe, leading to a sustained surge in interest in Japanese language studies and cultural exchange. Beyond the realm of entertainment, Japan's enduring status as a world leader in automotive technology, precision robotics, and cutting-edge high-tech innovation ensures that the language remains a crucial tool for international business, engineering, and scientific collaboration.

The history of the Japanese language is characterized by long periods of deliberate isolation followed by eras of rapid and enthusiastic assimilation of foreign influences. From the ancient adoption of Chinese writing and Buddhism to the modern, high-speed influx of English and other European loanwords, the language has always proved remarkably adaptive and resilient. Yet, at its core, Japanese remains a deeply traditional and nuanced tongue, serving as the essential and irreplaceable key to understanding the profound philosophy, long history, and unique aesthetics of the Japanese people as they navigate the complexities of the 21st century.
fra:
French is a global Romance language that originated in northern France and has since become an official language in 29 countries across five different continents. Universally recognized as the language of diplomacy, high philosophy, and the fine arts, French has played a central and defining role in the development of Western civilization and remains a vital tool for international communication today. As of the 2026 Ethnologue 29th Edition, it is notable for being one of the fastest-growing languages in the world, primarily driven by rapid demographic growth and increasing urbanization across Francophone Africa.

Historically, French achieved unprecedented prominence as the premier European lingua franca during the 17th and 18th centuries, serving as the preferred language of the aristocracy, the international scientific community, and the courts of Europe. It was the essential language of the Enlightenment, the medium through which thinkers like Voltaire, Rousseau, and Montesquieu disseminated their revolutionary ideas on democracy, secularism, and human rights. This immense intellectual heritage is still clearly reflected in the language's status as a primary working language for almost all major international organizations, including the United Nations, the European Union, and the International Olympic Committee.

French culture is globally synonymous with elegance, sophistication, and intellectualism, from its world-renowned culinary traditions and high-end fashion industry to its storied history in cinema, literature, and music. The 'Francophonie,' an international organization of French-speaking nations, works tirelessly to promote the language and celebrate its diverse cultural expressions, honoring the unique varieties of French spoken in places as diverse as Quebec, Senegal, Switzerland, and Vietnam. This global community ensures that French is not merely a language of historical prestige but a dynamic, diverse, and rapidly evolving medium for contemporary global life.

As the population of Africa continues its massive projected growth into the mid-21st century, the number of French speakers is expected to increase more significantly than almost any other major language. This profound demographic shift is repositioning French as a crucial language for economic development, regional cooperation, and cultural innovation across the African continent. Whether valued as a language of high historical prestige or recognized as a growing and energetic global force, French remains an indispensable part of our shared human history and an essential cultural and economic tool for the modern, interconnected world of 2026.
